name: SaaS Founder
description: Essential knowledge for SaaS founders — metrics, fundraising, pricing psychology, growth frameworks, and common pitfalls.

facts.text: LTV:CAC ratio should be at least 3:1. Below 3:1 means you're spending too much to acquire customers relative to their value. Above 5:1 often means you're underinvesting in growth.
Net Dollar Retention (NDR) above 120% means you grow even if you stop acquiring new customers. Best-in-class SaaS companies (Snowflake, Datadog) hit 130%+. Below 100% means your bucket is leaking.
Monthly logo churn above 5% is a crisis — you're replacing half your customer base every year. For SMB SaaS, target <3% monthly. For enterprise, target <1% monthly (or <10% annual).
CAC payback period should be under 18 months. Over 24 months and you have a capital efficiency problem. Under 12 months is excellent and means you can reinvest faster.

The Rule of 40: your revenue growth rate + profit margin should exceed 40%. At $10M ARR growing 60% with -15% margins, you're at 45% — healthy. This is the benchmark investors use to evaluate SaaS efficiency.

Gross margin for SaaS should be 70-85%. If yours is below 65%, you either have heavy infrastructure costs or services revenue dragging it down. Investors care about this because it determines how much revenue drops to the bottom line at scale.

Magic Number measures sales efficiency: (Net New ARR × 4) / Sales & Marketing Spend of prior quarter. Above 0.75 means you should invest more in sales. Below 0.5 means your go-to-market is inefficient.

ARR ≠ revenue. ARR is the annualized value of your recurring subscriptions right now. Revenue is what you actually collected. Mixing these up in a pitch deck will kill your credibility with sophisticated investors.
Pre-seed: $500K-$2M at $5-10M cap, typically from angels/pre-seed funds. Seed: $2-5M at $10-25M cap. Series A: $8-20M at $40-100M+ valuation, requires $1M+ ARR with clear path to $10M.

SAFE notes (Simple Agreement for Future Equity) are standard for pre-seed/seed. Use post-money SAFEs (YC standard) so you know exactly how much dilution you're taking. A $1M SAFE on a $10M post-money cap = 10% dilution.

Dilution math: expect to sell 15-25% at each round. After seed + A + B, founders typically own 30-45%. If you're giving up more than 25% per round, you're either raising too much or your valuation is too low.

Revenue-based financing (Pipe, Capchase, Lighter Capital) lets you borrow against ARR without dilution. Good for bridging between rounds or funding growth without giving up equity. Typical cost: 6-12% of capital.
Most VCs pass. The median VC sees 1,000+ deals/year and invests in 5-10. Don't take rejection personally — it's a numbers game. But if 50+ investors pass, the problem is your business, not bad luck.
Price anchoring: always show your most expensive plan first. Users perceive the middle tier as reasonable by comparison. This alone can increase average deal size 15-25%.

The decoy effect: add a plan that's intentionally bad value to make your target plan look great. If Basic is $29 and Pro is $79, add a 'Plus' at $69 with fewer features than Pro. Most buyers will pick Pro.

Usage-based pricing (Stripe, Twilio model) aligns cost with value and reduces churn, but makes revenue less predictable. Hybrid is often best: base platform fee + usage. This gives you a revenue floor with upside.

Annual contracts paid upfront are a cash flow weapon. Offer 15-20% discount for annual vs monthly. You get 10+ months of cash immediately, improve NDR, and reduce churn (annual churn is always lower than monthly×12).

Price increases are the highest-leverage growth move. A 10% price increase on existing customers drops straight to the bottom line. Most SaaS companies underprice by 30-50%. If no one complains about price, you're too cheap.

Product-Led Growth (PLG) works when: the product can be tried without sales assistance, value is experienced quickly (under 5 minutes), and the end user has buying power or strong influence. Slack, Figma, Notion are PLG. Enterprise security tools are not.

PLG metrics that matter: activation rate (% of signups who hit the 'aha moment'), time-to-value, PQL conversion rate (product-qualified lead → paid). If activation is below 25%, fix onboarding before spending on acquisition.

Enterprise sales cycle: 3-9 months. Land with a department (50-seat deal), expand to division, then company-wide. Don't try to sell company-wide on day one. The land-and-expand motion accounts for 30%+ of revenue at top SaaS companies.

Sales-led growth: when ACV (annual contract value) exceeds $15-25K, you need salespeople. Below that, the math rarely works — a $150K/year AE needs to close 10+ deals at $25K ACV to justify their cost.

The T2D3 benchmark: after reaching $2M ARR, triple revenue two years in a row, then double three years in a row. This gets you to ~$100M ARR in 5 years. Very few companies achieve this — it's the venture-scale bar.

Unit economics formula: LTV = ARPU × Gross Margin / Monthly Churn Rate. If ARPU is $100/mo, gross margin is 80%, and monthly churn is 3%, LTV = $100 × 0.80 / 0.03 = $2,667. CAC must be well below this.

Contribution margin per customer should be positive by month 12-18. If you're subsidizing customers beyond that, your pricing model is broken. Free tiers are fine but track how many convert and what they cost you to serve.
Customer acquisition channels have diminishing returns. What works at $1M ARR (founder-led sales, content marketing) breaks at $10M ARR. Plan your next channel before the current one saturates.
Churn is the silent killer. A 5% monthly churn means you need to replace 46% of your revenue every year just to stay flat. Reducing churn by 1% often has more impact than increasing new sales by 10%.

Building for enterprise too early kills startups. Enterprise means SOC2, SSO, SCIM, custom contracts, 6-month sales cycles, and support SLAs. Don't add these until you have 10+ enterprise prospects asking and willing to pay.

The 'one more feature' trap: customers ask for features, but what they really want is outcomes. The company that ships 3 features that work perfectly beats the company that ships 30 mediocre ones every time.
Discounting to close deals trains your market to expect discounts. Instead of dropping price, add value: extra onboarding, training, extended trial. Protect your pricing integrity — it compounds.

Founder-led sales is mandatory until $1-2M ARR. No one sells your product better than you. You also learn what customers actually need. Hiring salespeople before understanding your sales process is burning money.
Burn multiple: Net Burn / Net New ARR. Under 1.5x is efficient, 1.5-2x is okay, over 2x means you're burning too fast relative to growth. In a downturn, investors focus on this metric above all else.

Expansion revenue is the easiest revenue. Selling more to existing happy customers costs 5-7x less than acquiring new ones. Build your pricing to have natural expansion triggers: more seats, more usage, more features.

Quick Ratio = (New MRR + Expansion MRR) / (Churned MRR + Contraction MRR). Above 4 is excellent. Below 2 means growth is fragile. This is the single best metric for understanding the health of your revenue engine.

Don't optimize for vanity metrics: total signups, page views, downloads. Optimize for activation, retention, and revenue. A startup with 100 paying users who love you beats one with 100K signups and no engagement.

The best SaaS companies have negative churn — they grow faster from existing customers than they lose from churned ones. This only happens with strong expansion revenue and low logo churn. It's the holy grail.
